language_register: Formal mid-nineteenth-century American literary English, modelled on the prose of Webster, Clay, and the better Washington correspondents of the period. The register is elevated without being ornate—periodic sentences balanced against shorter declarative ones, Latinate diction for political and legal discourse, Anglo-Saxon directness for physical action and private feeling. Modern readers must feel the weight of the age without requiring a glossary.
vocabulary_constraints: Prefer Latinate vocabulary for educated, politically active characters (senators, cabinet officers, lawyers): 'remonstrate' over 'complain,' 'approbation' over 'approval,' 'exigency' over 'emergency.'
Reserve Germanic, monosyllabic diction for moments of physical urgency, raw emotion, or vernacular speech: a man does not 'expire' in a brawl—he dies.
Use period-accurate currency: dollars and cents are standard by 1841; avoid 'pounds' except in reference to British transactions or older characters recalling earlier custom.
Distances are rendered in miles, furlongs, or days' travel—never kilometres or metric units of any kind.

Political terminology must reflect 1841 usage: 'the Administration' means Tyler's executive; 'the party' is never used without specifying Whig or Democrat; 'Congress' refers to the body, not individual members.

Avoid the word 'president' as a casual honorific in direct address; characters say 'Mr. President,' 'Your Excellency,' or 'sir' depending on the speaker's standing and the degree of deference intended.

Class distinctions in vocabulary are mandatory: a Virginia planter employs the full arsenal of classical allusion and courtly circumlocution; a Washington boarding-house keeper speaks in plain American idiom with regional colour.
Newspapers and pamphlets are the era's mass media; characters quote, cite, and argue from printed sources—never from rumour alone without acknowledging its provenance.

Legal and constitutional language must be precise and period-authentic: 'devolve upon,' 'discharge the powers and duties,' 'in the case of removal, death, resignation, or inability' are the operative phrases of the succession debate.
Avoid all contractions in formal written documents, speeches, and official correspondence rendered in the narrative; contractions are permissible in private dialogue among intimates.
forbidden_terms: at the end of the day (idiomatic)
dialogue_style_guide: Dialogue must be the primary instrument of character revelation and political manoeuvre. Educated characters—senators, cabinet members, Virginia gentry—speak in full, architecturally complete sentences, deploying subordinate clauses as weapons of precision or evasion. They address one another by title and surname in public ('Mr. Clay,' 'Mr. Webster'), by surname alone among intimates, and by Christian name only in moments of unusual warmth or unusual crisis. Emotion is conveyed obliquely: a man who is furious speaks with preternatural calm; a man who is afraid becomes more formal, not less. Working characters—clerks, servants, tradesmen—speak in shorter, more direct constructions, with regional idiom permitted but never caricatured. Women of the planter and political class are articulate, ironic, and fully capable of the political sentence; they are not decorative. Interruptions, silences, and the failure to answer a direct question are as expressive as any speech. Period oaths and exclamations ('By Heaven,' 'I'll be damned,' 'Upon my honour') are used sparingly and only when dramatically earned.
narrative_voice_calibration: Close third-person, rotating among three to five principal viewpoints, each chapter anchored to a single consciousness. The voice is intimate but not confessional—modelled on Hilary Mantel's Wolf Hall in its willingness to inhabit a mind without announcing that it is doing so. Sentences vary: long, clause-laden periods for political reflection and interior reckoning; short declaratives for physical action and shock. The narrator is not ironic at the characters' expense but is never naive about their self-deceptions. Emotional temperature runs cool on the surface and volcanic beneath—the prose registers what characters will not say aloud. Historical context is delivered through perception and action, never through authorial summary.
example_dialogue_snippets: 'Mr. President—if I may call you so—the Cabinet is of one mind on this point.' / 'You may call me what you will, Mr. Webster. The Constitution has already settled the question to my satisfaction.'
'They mean to break him,' Wise said, refilling his glass. 'Clay cannot endure a man who will not be broken.' / 'Then Clay has not yet made the acquaintance of John Tyler.'

'Sir, the bill has passed both chambers. Your veto will be seen as a declaration of war upon your own party.' / 'I was not aware, Mr. Ewing, that I had pledged my conscience to any party as the price of this office.'
'He signed nothing, gave nothing away—sat there in that great chair as though he had been born to it.' / 'Perhaps he has been. Virginia has a long memory for such things.'
